items.question: Who relies on the chain rule when training neural networks?
items.answer: Every practitioner using gradient-based training—frameworks apply the chain rule automatically during backpropagation to compute loss gradients for all parameters.

items.question: What does the one-variable chain rule state for h(x) = f(g(x))?
items.answer: h′(x) = f′(g(x))·g′(x): multiply the derivative of the outer function evaluated at the inner output by the derivative of the inner function.

items.question: When do you apply the chain rule in a training step?
items.answer: During the backward pass after the forward pass: each layer propagates upstream loss sensitivity by multiplying with its local Jacobian or scalar derivative.

items.question: Where does the chain rule appear in a PyTorch training loop?
items.answer: In loss.backward(), which walks the autograd graph from the loss node backward, chaining local gradients until every leaf parameter has a .grad value.

items.question: Why is the chain rule essential for deep learning instead of finite differences?
items.answer: It decomposes a deep composition into cheap local factors, giving exact parameter gradients in time comparable to one forward pass—impractical with per-parameter finite differencing.

items.question: How do you differentiate f(x) = e^{sin(x)} using the chain rule?
items.answer: Let u = sin x, f(u) = e^u. Then f′(u)·u′ = e^{sin x}·cos x—outer exponential derivative times inner sine derivative.
